title: ML Interview Study Guide
subtitle: MLE / Research Scientist interview preparation. Follow this order.
note: Each phase builds on the previous. Don't skip ahead — interviewers probe foundations first. Agents and LLMs can be reviewed anytime since they require less math.

phases.label: Linear Algebra
phases.why: Every ML algorithm is linear algebra in disguise. This phase unlocks everything else.
phases.interview_tip: For any matrix operation, be ready to: (1) write the equation, (2) explain dimensions, (3) give a numerical 2x2 example.

phases.label: Probability & Statistics
phases.why: Loss functions, model selection, and uncertainty all come from probability. You need this before understanding why gradient descent converges.
phases.interview_tip: Bias-variance is asked in every ML interview. Derive it, give the formula, and give a concrete example (high bias = underfitting, high variance = overfitting).

phases.label: Optimization
phases.why: Training = optimization. You cannot discuss any model without understanding gradient descent, Adam, and the pathologies (vanishing/exploding).
drill_topics: Vanishing gradient — why it happens mathematically
phases.interview_tip: Adam is almost always asked at top companies. Know: momentum term, RMS term, bias correction, and why it beats SGD on sparse gradients.

phases.label: Regularization
phases.why: Directly connected to overfitting. Comes up in every interview when they ask 'how would you improve this model?'
drill_topics: L1 vs L2 — equations and geometry
phases.interview_tip: The geometric explanation of L1 sparsity (diamond vs circle constraint) is a favourite hard question. Draw it if you have a whiteboard.

phases.label: Traditional ML Algorithms
phases.why: Breadth coverage. Interviewers at big tech still ask these. Also forms your mental model for comparing algorithms.
drill_topics: KNN — distance metrics + curse of dimensionality
t-SNE vs UMAP — when to use each
Feature engineering — encoding, scaling, interaction terms
phases.interview_tip: Don't memorise. For each algorithm: (1) when to use it, (2) key hyperparameter, (3) failure mode. That's what interviewers want.

phases.label: Deep Learning Core
phases.why: Non-negotiable for MLE/R&D roles. Backprop + attention are the two most-asked deep learning questions.
drill_topics: Backprop — chain rule, write update for one weight
Batch normalisation — equations + why it helps
Scaled dot-product attention — derive the equation
ResNets — skip connections and why they help
phases.interview_tip: For attention: write Q, K, V — softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) V. Interviewers will ask what happens without the sqrt(d_k) scaling. Answer: dot products grow large, softmax saturates, gradients vanish.

phases.label: Deep Architectures
phases.why: For R&D roles, you need depth beyond transformers. VAEs, LSTMs, and transfer learning come up frequently.
drill_topics: LSTM gates — forget, input, output (write equations)
Transfer learning — fine-tuning vs feature extraction
phases.interview_tip: VAE reparameterisation trick is a top R&D interview question: z = mu + eps * sigma where eps ~ N(0,1). Explain why we can't backprop through sampling directly.

phases.label: ML Systems Design
phases.why: Chip Huyen's book covers the production side. System design rounds are standard at FAANG. Data leakage has failed many senior candidates.
drill_topics: Class imbalance — SMOTE, class weights, threshold moving
Concept drift — detection (PSI, KS test) and remediation
Time series — correct train/val split (no future leakage)
Health AI — regulatory constraints, label noise
phases.interview_tip: System design: always start with 'what is the business metric?' then work backwards to the ML metric. Interviewers penalise candidates who jump to modelling without questioning the problem framing.

phases.label: LLM & Agents
phases.why: Hot area — almost guaranteed questions at any company doing AI products. Less foundational math, more architectural understanding. Can revisit anytime.
drill_topics: RLHF pipeline — SFT → reward model → PPO
DPO vs RLHF — why DPO skips the reward model
Agent memory types — semantic, episodic, procedural
phases.interview_tip: For LLM questions: always mention trade-offs. LoRA is cheaper but may underperform full fine-tuning for large distribution shifts. RAG is cheap but retrieval quality is the bottleneck.

phases.label: Framework Studio
phases.why: Practical coding fluency. Shows interviewers you can ship, not just theorise. Do this alongside Phase 9 or after.
drill_topics: OpenAI Agents SDK — Agent + Runner.run() pattern
phases.interview_tip: For coding rounds: narrate your choices. 'I'm using LangGraph here because the workflow has conditional branching and I need persistent state across turns.' That's the answer they want.

quick_reference.if_you_have_1_week: Phases 1, 3, 6 only — linear algebra, optimization, deep learning core. These are the most common interview blockers.
quick_reference.if_you_have_2_weeks: Phases 1–6 — adds statistics, regularisation, and traditional ML breadth.
quick_reference.daily_routine: 30 min drill (Core Drill or Book Exercises) + 15 min cheat sheet review. Weak spots auto-surface — trust the system.
